---
title: "Bull Trout Status and Threats"
subtitle: "Multi-Population Viability Analysis"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

<!-- =====================================================================
     COLLABORATORS: Feel free to edit any text in this file.
     Please DO NOT modify anything inside ```{r} ... ``` code blocks —
     those generate content automatically from the model.
     ===================================================================== -->

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(here)
fit      <- readRDS(here("Results", "MPVA_Fit_D1_2_M1_14_R1_14.RDS"))
n_pop    <- fit$data_list$nPop
n_core   <- fit$data_list$nCore
yr_range <- range(fit$DatYears)
```
Authors: 
Timothy Cline, Montana State University (corresponding author timothy.cline2@montana.edu)
Clint Muhlfeld, USGS - Northern Rocky Mountain Science Center 
Robert Al-Chokhachy, USGS - Northern Rocky Mountain Science Center


Bull trout (*Salvelinus confluentus*) are a cold-water obligate salmonid
native to western North America and listed as Threatened under the U.S.
Endangered Species Act. This site presents a hierarchical state-space
population model developed through a collaboration between the US Geological Survey,
US Fish and Wildlife Service, Montana Fish Wildlife and Parks, and Montana State University.
The goal is to synthesize redd count monitoring data across the Northern Rocky
Mountains and assess population status, threats, and vulnerability to inform conservation strategies.

---

## What this model does

The model integrates long-term redd count time series (`r yr_range[1]`–`r yr_range[2]`) from
**`r n_pop` local populations** nested within **`r n_core` core areas**
to estimate:

- **Population-specific growth rates** and how they vary with environmental conditions
- **Density dependence** at the population level
- **Effects of climate** (summer streamflow, August water temperature) on population growth
- **Biotic threats** from non-native trout and lake trout
- **Habitat quality** indicators (adfluvial life history access, valley bottom extent, W95 winter flood frequency, road density)

---

## Site structure

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
### [Data](data.qmd)

Explore the raw redd count time series and environmental covariate data for
each core area and local population. Use the right-side table of contents to
navigate directly to the populations under your purview.
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### [Model Results](index.qmd)

View fitted population trajectories, observed vs. fitted redd counts,
covariate effect estimates (including population-specific random slopes),
and model variance components.
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### About the model

The model is a hierarchical state-space Ricker model fitted with
[TMB](https://github.com/kaskr/adcomp) (Template Model Builder).
Random effects are integrated out via the Laplace approximation.
Parameter uncertainty is propagated via the delta method.
:::

::::

---

## Species background

Bull trout require cold, clear, complex streams for spawning and rearing.
They are possibly sensitive to:

| Threat | Mechanism |
|--------|-----------|
| Warming water temperatures | Upper thermal tolerance ~13–15 °C for spawning adults; lethal above ~20 °C |
| Altered hydrology | Peak flow timing and winter flood frequency affect gravel incubation success |
| Non-native trout | Competitive displacement and hybridization (brown trout, brook trout); predation (lake trout) |
| Road density | Fine sediment delivery to spawning gravels; barrier culverts |
| Lack or loss of migratory access | Adfluvial populations (lake-resident adults) have higher productivity |

---

*Model version 1.14 · Data version 1.2 · Source code at
[github.com/timothycline/BullTroutSSP](https://github.com/timothycline/BullTroutSSP)*
